speaker: interviewer
text: Mister Steinberg, you were on a death march from Radom--

speaker: interviewee
text: Tomaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tomaszow. You said a third of the population was either killed--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or died.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or died. How long did this-- that march last?

speaker: interviewee
text: It lasted, in my estimation, maybe three days. You know, it was 100 kilometers. Maybe a little more than how I [? call it, ?] [INAUDIBLE], But three days walking, day and night. We stopped only for-- maybe to lay down, to rest for-- but it was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food did they give you? You said you were fed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they gave us soup, coffee. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Gave us soup or coffee now. They-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- and bread, a little tiny piece of bread. Where did they make the coffee and the-- the soup?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had-- they had already prepared or something now, before they came down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it warm? Was it cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably cold, you know? It looked like mud-- mud-- mud coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you arrived to Tomaszow, what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't go in-- we-- we wouldn't go in to the-- to the place. We already found out that Jews are being killed, that Jews are being gassed. We found out because some people from there escaped from Treblinka and from Meiderich. And then they caught them and they brought them back to Radom, to the-- to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we heard from them the news, that of the most awful things is going on. We escape from there. Awful, the-- the-- going on. I know it's going to be. I said, all my family's already killed already.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we saw trains of people were [? fly-- ?] [? would ?] [? freight ?] [? to Radom ?] into-- to east, because they were from Holland, they were from here to different places. So we knew something was going on. But these Jews told us something very bad's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went-- when we went-- we went to Tomaszow. And they took us into this-- it was a hanger from an airplane, hanger. Where they make-- where they make airplanes. I recall, just like now, that it had a glass roof. Ceiling was all glass. So we figured-- we told-- we were told before that they'd take us into a place not glass, with little-- tiny little ducts and the gas comes out [? now. ?] And they kill you. And so the Jews, the party, we wouldn't go in.

speaker: interviewee
text: There wasn't enough guards, no. So the captain who was in charge of the transport, he says, I know what you're afraid, that you maybe get killed. No, I know all about it. But let me go in. I'll stay with you. I will smell this-- the-- [INAUDIBLE]. The SS men say that. You can go inside. The reason we went in there is because we waited for a train to come in. This hanger was right near a train station outside the city. We laid on the floor. We laid there. We slept on the floor. We were there-- we were there about, well, two days or three nights, three days, now. And we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- until we got there, until we were put on a train-- and that-- we were put on the train. And this time, it was an unusual train. The train was all blacked out now, with the ceiling, you know. The small little window, and the window had chicken wires, you know. And as soon as they put in a tremendous amount of people inside, now, we couldn't lay down. We couldn't stand up. We couldn't do nothing. We had to be close to each other. It was warm-- we were-- it was-- it was right before the fall-- fall season now. So it was very sweaty inside now. They had some chlorine on the floor, for-- people were urinating, you know. And everybody was trying to grasp for-- for air, going to that window, go and try to smell, get air. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they locked up the door. And we heard them put the handle over. And here we go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we heard a big sound from the train, going. And we go through mountains that go to each other. Why-- we went-- we went to different places, [? other, ?] why? Because they had to go in safe places. People shouldn't see them, what they're doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and we shouldn't be bombed by somebody. [? I know. ?] They went through places now that they can travel. It wasn't a direct route. So we went to Czechoslovakia, I remember I saw. Bratislava, I saw. I remember I saw-- we went to Austria and I saw a city called, Linz. We walked in. And we were begging to the window. We were begging please, please help us. Now, we saw nuns, now, standing at a train. Please help us. Please help us. No. Nobody came to our help. And I noticed one time I did see a nun was trying to give out, give-- put water. But the Germans wouldn't let her do it. They chased her away. Because I was able-- they-- in the-- in the train, I stand on somebody's shoulder. And somebody-- I looked out and saw what's going on. Until, in the morning, we arrived at Godforsaken place, Auschwitz. And we saw the sign outside, you know, [GERMAN]. And we saw people walking around there. They looked like dead people. And we saw people. We saw electric wires, we saw. We saw like, the biggest jail in the whole world. It was very far away from the city. We called, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear that name before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never heard of it. In the ghetto, we heard that people who did any crimes now, before the war-- I don't know. I never-- I never heard of it. I-- you know, it was all-- then we-- we arrived, the train. We saw people near the gate there. The plane-- the train stopped right in the front of the camp. We saw music. People was playing music at the gate, now. [? We ?] [? did not know ?] [? the ?] music they were were playing there. That-- the train stayed approximate, I would say, maybe about 500 to 1,000 feet away from the gate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they told, everybody out! Out! They were beating us now, with sticks, you know, and [? basically, ?] everybody out! You know. There was no-- there was nothing. Just a suitcase. Maybe not you know, suitcases, but nothing. Just out! Out! And we went out. We went out. We stayed in line. We-- my brother, my father, and my-- and the selection was performed. My brother and my father and myself, plus my brother's-- a brother, a cousin, a few family-- in the family stayed the same line. My brother was what-- a-- a head shorter than me. Maybe more than a head. And so we lift him up. We had to lift up, now. When Dr. Mengele, I remember of the-- the man with the white coat, with that policeman, with a-- with a few SS, the one-- one kapo with a red hat. He had a stick in his hand now. He wore a big armband here that-- with a-- with a-- with a Jewish star. When he went over to each line, and he checked out each line. We were showing our bodies, that we have no sores, no. We're like, naked now. But no sores, no-- no any kind of diseases, no. And he picked out this one, they go over here. This one, go over here. This one, go over here. Go on the-- on the train. Go on the-- on the wagon. Go over here now. And that's how-- I lift up my brother. We lift up. And he went, fortunately, OK. Next. And all this line we were-- we were my brothers-- brother, my-- my-- my cousin, now, that particular-- 10 line. 10 people in a line. We all were saved. And afterward, they took out maybe, on that trip-- I don't know exactly how many they took out now. Quite a bit. And all the people, there was a few women there they picked up in the road, you know. There was a few woman. [? Every-- ?] [? kill-- ?] they find a baby. A few babies they found, that are hid-- women hid in their-- you know, between their bosoms, baby, tiny baby. Bring them all up and out. And they are all marched away inside. Which we never saw again.

speaker: interviewer
text: We remained there a whole day. If I'm not mistaken, I think another day. We were there but one day or two days. I can't remember. But I know every minute I know when we going to get out of here? When we going to get out of here, you know? Just outdoors, you were staying?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were outside the gate from Auschwitz. And they were writing our numbers down. My number is 262-- 26203. The number-- the number they write down, the number, my brother's number. They were writing our numbers down. And they put us back on the same train. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they disconnected a lot of-- a few-- a few cars because not as many people [? already. ?] And the train took off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me take you back for a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one German, they gave us some food. A piece of sausage, now, some bread now. One German walked over to me-- and to us, not to me. And he said, OK, eating right now-- he says, eating sausage. [GERMAN], [? from ?] [? Jews. ?] And then he gave us-- every a piece of-- a piece of-- of soap. It had R-J-F, [GERMAN]. I'll never forget that, though.

speaker: interviewer
text: This what they made in Auschwitz. And that's how we went in the inside. What did you want to ask me? Inside where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the-- to the cattle car. To the-- to the-- to the-- to the train again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the soap?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the soap? We were supposed to use it, you know? Because we know when we come to the camp, we're supposed to, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a piece of that soap?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. For a long time, I had it, yeah, yeah. For a long time, a piece of-- and then we used it. And I was trying to keep it for a long time. I-- after the war, I think I threw it away.

words.text: For a long time, a piece of--

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to take you back before you left Radom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where your stepmother and younger brother is?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never heard them again. Not only them, the whole city completely, nobody knew what happened to them. But we heard, while we were in the Radom camp, you know, they were all put [? through ?] the camp, that-- that Treblinka, they were all-- they were waiting about three, four hours, and all killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So now you left Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: We left Auschwitz now. And from Auschwitz, the train was going, and going, and going, you know. Until we came to a city called Vaihingen. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was a real camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never heard of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Vaihingen always happened near the Neckar. In Germany there's a-- there's a river called Neckar. So all these camps where I was now was now on the Neckar.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew it was in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, they spoke German. It was clean city, you know. We heard the bombs going off. I mean, we know it was Germany, sure. There was German writings we saw when the train went through, you know. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of camp was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't a camp. Oh, I want to go back to Auschwitz, before I forget. Another thing I should never forget when I was in Auschwitz, now, the smell. It had the most unusual smell. I have never smelled this in my life, no. It had a tremendous-- we saw the chimney. We saw a big, huge chimney that was white in the bottom. It came to narrow. And I never saw smoke coming out, no. But I did smell something. While I was staying there, there was an unusual odor. And I want to stress that now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find out what that came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never asked. Never asked. It smelled terrible. A different kind of smell like I never smelled before. It wasn't-- believe me, it wasn't steak. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you left Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left Auschwitz now. We-- we-- we-- the train must have gone two or three days. Because it was-- they stopped every minute. And there were bombs, you know, there were different kind of interruptions on the road you know. It was going back and forth, back and forth, you know, the train. Because for different rails, different connections, you know, until we arrived to a camp called Vaihingen. It was a brand new camp. It was just built. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were-- this was a camp I shall never forget. Very, very bad. No food. you-- you-- we were given a tiny piece of bread. It was a torturous camp, you know. It was a tiny piece of bread at night, to eat it tomorrow morning, you know. So my father, we were there. My father was there. My brother was there. Maybe had a tiny piece of-- he made me a little bag. He made me a little bag, little piece of string. He said, look-- me-- listen [? that, ?] take the piece of bread and put it in that little piece of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --bag, you know, he says. And then, he says, now, you eat that tomorrow morning, you know. And keep it when you sleep. Hold it like now, so nobody can cut it from you. Because there's people who cut it at night. And they'll come by-- over with a knife and cut it, you know, and try to steal it from you. So sleep with your-- with your hand holding on the string. I-- and we used to go out at night. They used to assign so much-- so many pieces of bread to give each halfling, which means each-- each prison-- prisoner. So people used to die at night. So in the morning, you know, if somebody died, you know, we used to bring him out dead, holding him. When they counted, you know, we had two or three people dead. Now we got their bread. Then, after they wrote down 60 or 80 people, we're in the barracks now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So instead of 58, no, yeah? So we got the piece of bread. And then we would divide it more. And then we reported they were dead and they took them away. So this is how we got more money, and more-- more-- more bread, you know? I want to ask you, how did it feel to be with your father and with your brother, and not knowing from day to day, what's going to happen to any of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, nobody in that camp, nobody began to know who we are. Because they were looking for brothers. They were looking for fathers. They were looking to-- to kill.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were looking already. They was dig around, any brothers here? Any sisters here? Any-- any uncles here?

speaker: interviewee
text: They wanted to diminish any kind of relationship, any kind of family, they shouldn't-- you know, they want to kill them. So my brother and I had a whistle. And this whistle-- we know my father and my brother and I had a whistle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we used to whistle so he knew we were here. Or if one's different-- a different barrack, you know? And that whistle, if I give the whistle, he used to give me the-- whistle-- the whistle back the same way. And that's how we knew we were alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: But nobody knew about Steinberg, nobody knew about [? Schlumer, ?] or [? Mendel, ?] or Chaim. Nobody knew it. It was a dead-- dead issue. We became the dead people of the world living, [? but with a ?] whistle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did not stay in the same barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we stayed-- sometime we stayed and sometime we didn't stay. It's all according what they-- who-- who-- what they assigned us, no? It-- it was better if we wouldn't stay in the same barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because if they would stay in the same barracks, you know, there would be a close contact. You know? And then they probably would have kill us. Did you work in this new camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: This camp was a torturous camp. We took one stone from here and put it there. One stone from there, put over here. Then they took us out now to a city where they bombed, you know, to pick up the ruins, you know, and look for any things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I never forget, I went to a city called Heilbronn. A lot of watches were there. I couldn't even steal a watch. Because you know why? We had no use for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and they-- they was-- we used to-- we used to work in the fields, now, making foxholes. We used to work in the mines, you know, Some kind of mines, you know, we were working.

words.text: Some kind of mines, you know,

speaker: interviewer
text: We used to help-- help--

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: We used to help doing certain things like fixing the-- the barracks, you know. What with electrical matters, fixed the lights, you know? They-- they had people working, keeping people-- people busy, you know? But every other minute, we used to appell. Go outside in the street, you know? in the-- and the-- and the platz now. Take off your hats now, [INAUDIBLE] off, [INAUDIBLE] on, [INAUDIBLE], off. It was going on for hours, hours. And we didn't have no hair. So we used to do that, you know. And if somebody-- and a guy used to come in like, in charge of the Army SS. You know, the SS, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to take off our hat 50 times, like a-- like a-- give him some kind of salute. And then we used to stay there, at attention, if somebody came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what do you call it? Like when the high echelon from the-- from the high officers, you know. What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: This camp, you know, I stayed probably-- probably a few months. This camp was very, very bad. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any friends from Radom--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still there were-- there were friends there. But like I mentioned before, you know, there was no such a thing as friends. You know, everybody was from [? Sobno. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody was trying to survive. Many times I laid down on the bunk, you know, and I cried. And with my brother, my younger brother, you know, I cried. And I said, how long-- I said-- many times I laid in the bunk, you know? I took my shoes as my pillow. My two wooden shoes were my pillow. And my little blanket they gave me was my little blanket, you know? It was cold like, I would imagine [? the ?] [? word ?] hell. So cold, you know? In Germany it's very cold in certain times of the year.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's how it-- and-- and I laid down on the bunk and cried, you know. And I only prayed one thing. I prayed now-- I prayed to God to take my life. I was wondering, when is the end, it's going to stop? Please, I says now, why did you choose me, I said, to do that? Now get my life and forget about it. But I can't take it no more. So where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there-- well, this-- this-- then we went to a city, to a camp called, Unterriexingen. And if Vaihingen was bad, Unterriexingen was a thousand times worse. The-- out there-- the war was getting very close. The Americans, they were getting very close to coming in to-- to Germany. We laid on the bunk, you know. We used to hear the bullets going. The-- the-- the airplanes were attacking. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- the Germans were hitting us terrible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I was-- and I only weighed maybe 70-some pounds. I weighed maybe close to 80 pounds, I weighed. I was so skinny. You can't imagine. My father and my brother, we were-- my-- my brother helped us quite a bit. I want to mention that too, now. He was became a [? yarner. ?] He fixed the Germans', the SS, their socks. He knows how to fix socks. That the hole in the heel, or something else, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave him some yarn and a big needle. And he-- he did that. He learned how to-- you know-- and they used to give him bread, you know. And he used to-- he used to help.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I want to stress that. Because without his helping, we probably would have been dead. And-- and then he also used to catch rats, my brother. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We used to call him the [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You know? But-- And what did he do with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Catch-- you know, the rats, there, you know, in Germany. There were a lot of rats you know. He used to always, now, take a-- a whip and have to catch-- catch the rats. This was-- but the most important thing that he was-- was he was-- what do you call it-- the yarn make-- that he fixed-- and that he was darning the socks now. This was a very big help for us now. Because they used-- they used to give him bread, now, the SS, you know. And they gave him shoes. Not shoes, no, coffee or soup, you know. And he was able to give it to us now. Because they shared together. So he was like a savior to us, the younger brother. He learned how to do that. He was a-- he was a very handy guy. And he was already about-- I was 19 already. He was about 17. He was very small, too, then. Very tiny.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: This camp mostly making the foxholes, work in the yard, work in-- in-- fixing-- taking one stone, another stone. Going out from the camp, going out, workinig in the-- in the muds, cleaning the-- cleaning the mud-- mud puddles, you know, from the water, you know? Working the fields, picking up corn. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Fixing-- Corn for what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Corn. For growing corn. Just the growing corn. So maybe then we used to help, I don't know, pick them up and-- and put them in bags, you know, someplace, you know. And then we-- we used to-- mostly it was-- was very manual, hard work you know. And if you don't eat nothing, it becomes even harder. They gave us this piece of bread at night, now, in order to eat it tomorrow morning, you know. A lot of people who ate it the same time didn't survive. Because in the morning when they gave them the black coffee, they needed the bread from last night. They didn't have it. They only drank black coffee. That's how they didn't make it. When you were in the corn fields, did you have a chance to eat some corn?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, my father, he stoled about four corns, put it under his shirts, you know. I cannot explain to you how unmercifully he was beaten, my father. He was skinny. He was trying to bring in three or four just for us, you know, just to eat. You know? [? He ?] [? had ?] [INAUDIBLE], they caught him doing that. He kept under his-- under his jacket he kept it, you know? Because-- And they beat him unmercifully.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In fact, a policeman, a Jewish policeman beat him, too. Kicked him and gave him in his whole stomach his guts went down right away, like a rupture. It gave him a rupture, he gave him, from kicking him right in the groin. This is something not to forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see when your father was beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how can I forget? How can I-- How can I not see? We were right there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I feel? Well, I felt like I mentioned in the beginning, you know, Jewish people are always passive, you know. They were never [? warriors, ?] you know, very like, never fighting back, you know. This was our mistake. This was our-- this was our misfortune in life now, that we didn't fight back, organize, you know. We didn't have the leaders we have now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe if we'd of had somebody tell us what to do, how to do it, maybe we would have done it, you know. We didn't have nobody to tell us. How I felt? I felt very bad. I felt I wanted to go over there and risk my life and kill the German. But how can I?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was afraid. My life was also something. I was only hoping one thing. Maybe tomorrow might be the day I will be-- we will be-- survive. Maybe tomorrow will be another day. Maybe tomorrow I'll be free. So this was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did this go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in this camp, you know, we had my nephew. My-- my cousin was there, Shaya. My brother's-- my father's brother was there. He died from diarrhea. My-- my-- my cousin, a few cousins died there. They all had diarrhea and died. You know, diarrhea. And this was going on until-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --1944. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: About November. November or December. And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe even-- no, no. Excuse me. It was January. You know why? Because I heard-- they were singing. Because it was-- it was already Christmas. Because I heard that the Germans were singing in the camp. I'll never forget that. They were singing, 'O Tanenbaum,' they were singing. This is something, I'll never forget that. 'O Tanenbaum.' They were all singing, in the sentries, 'O Tanenbaum.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was like December?

speaker: interviewee
text: December, yeah. And then in December, they took me out. They took us, another camp. My brother to a different camp. He went to another camp, you know. And I-- my mind was not normal to comprehend, nor to see, nor to worry, nor to-- I was-- I became like an animal. I didn't even know what was going on in the world now. I became to-- excuse my expression-- I was afraid to go at night, to the bathroom, from the camp, you know, because they said, if you're going to go at night to the bathroom, they will shoot you. So people-- especially me, I'm not talking about people-- I used to make in the same pot, now, and eat in the same pot now.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop for a moment.
